subject: Boots
method: full human/LLM read of transcript 046 (not the heuristic)
note: All 37 tiers below were confirmed to match the stored tierlists.json exactly. The 'why' field is new content extracted from the transcript. Boots mostly give utility/mobility rather than raw stats, so the best pick varies by character; rated by power when acquired.

Boots of Aid and Comfort.why: Healing a target also grants them 3 temp HP; weak alone but a core piece of the on-heal trio with Hellrider's Pride and Whispering Promise (Bless + Blade Ward + temp HP), and obtainable early — pre-buff the whole party by throwing a potion.

Boots of Speed.why: Bonus-action double movement every turn — a ton of mobility for any character without a better bonus-action use, great for closing distance or escaping a losing fight; slightly undercut by bonus-action jump and the Night Walkers, but you get it first.

Boots of Stormy Clamour.why: Inflicting any condition also applies 2 turns of Reverberation (-1 physical saves/stack, prone at 5 stacks); reverberation is a top-tier broken mechanic and these are the best way to stack it — radiating-orb clerics and tiger-heart barbarians abuse it, but anyone benefits.

Disintegrating Night Walkers.why: Misty Step once per short rest (the best mobility tool) plus immunity to web/entangle/ensnare and no slipping on grease/ice — everything you want from boots in one package; arguably the single most-equipped item in the game.

Vital Conduit Boots.why: 8 temp HP whenever you cast a concentration spell (even a cantrip); S-tier for Spore Druids, where a bug lets it fully restore the Spore shield for effectively infinite temp HP, and A-tier for other medium-armor casters.

Evasive Shoes.why: +1 acrobatics and +1 AC — a rare flat passive stat boost from the boot slot, so many parties equip it on a character lacking a specific boot need; that +1 AC can lift a low-AC monk/light-armor character to stop dragging down party defense.

Boots of Uninhibited Kushigo.why: Adds your Wisdom modifier to unarmed-attack damage — best-in-slot for every monk (doubling Wis-to-damage on an open-hand monk), a huge per-attack boost on a class that attacks many times a turn.

Helldusk Boots.why: Force-movement immunity, ignore difficult terrain, a bonus-action short-rest Misty Step with a no-save 28 fire explosion, AND once/rest auto-succeed a failed save — an incredible all-in-one defensive package, best-in-slot endgame boots.

Boots of Striding.why: One turn of momentum on casting a concentration spell, but the real draw is immunity to prone/push while concentrating — preventing the no-save concentration loss from being knocked over; great for Druid/cleric casters, though defense-only.

Linebreaker Boots.why: 3 turns of Wrath (+1 melee damage/stack, up to +7) when you dash in combat; needs bonus-action dash and melee weapons, so it shines on dagger Rogues and Stallion-barbarian/Rogue builds that dash to close anyway.

The Watersparkers.why: Standing in water electrifies it and grants 3 lightning charges/turn — combined with the Spark Swall ring (immunity to the shock) it's free, constant charge generation plus an area the AI avoids; the core item for any lightning-charge build.

Bonespike Boots.why: +5ft jump, +1 AC/saves while unarmored/shieldless, and a bonus-action Brutal Leap that knocks enemies prone for two turns (item save DC); excellent for unarmored Barbarians and defensive monks who want to leap onto enemies.

Boots of Psionic Movement.why: Fly as a bonus action once per long rest (works for any class, not just gith) — a strong, slot-free, flexible mobility option and an interesting alternative to the Night Walkers for parties that aren't going full-illithid.

Boots of Elemental Momentum.why: Momentum (2 turns, +movement) when you deal elemental spell damage; good mobility, but restricted to medium-armor casters (Tempest cleric, Druid, gith) and it wants you to lead with a damage spell, which isn't always ideal.

Hoarfrost Shoes.why: Can't fall prone on icy terrain — passive (unlike Boots of Striding) so it works for non-casters, but it doesn't stop enemy-forced prone; only worth it for an ice-surface party already running something like the Snowburst Ring.

Springstep Boots.why: 3 turns of momentum (+15ft movement) when you dash; decent extra mobility, but characters who bonus-action dash usually prefer the Linebreaker Boots' Wrath, since you're already moving far on a dash turn.

The Speedy Lightfeet.why: 3 lightning charges on dash (medium-armor); both halves are outclassed — Linebreaker Boots give better dash damage and the Watersparkers stack charges more easily — but useful for a ranged lightning-charge build (charges work on ranged attacks, Wrath doesn't).

Boots of Brilliance.why: Regain one Bardic Inspiration charge once per long rest; a useful resource for Bards, but it takes fiddly swap-in/swap-out micro to maximize, so most won't bother.

Boots of Persistence.why: Longstrider plus Freedom of Movement (difficult-terrain immunity and, crucially, immunity to paralysis and restrained); a fine peace-of-mind swap-in for hold-person fights, rolling several weak effects into one decent package.

Boots of Very Fast Blinking.why: At-will Misty Step (once/turn) with a brutal catch: you drop your armor, helmet, gloves, and cloak where you blink from; reasonable for unarmored Barbarians/monks and for infinite out-of-combat traversal if you tolerate re-equipping micro.

Spacehunt Boots.why: Dimension Door once per long rest; a relatively weak spell normally, but free it's a useful swap-in for late-game encounters where teleporting yourself (and allies) around matters.

Cinder Shoes.why: 2 turns of Heat when you burn an enemy, discharged for +1 fire damage/stack in an AOE; only relevant for a build centered on burning enemies (Alchemist's Fire / fire arrows), and Heat is a weak mechanic — mostly ignorable.

Mystra's Grace.why: Feather Fall at will; most parties already have it as a ritual spell, so it's only handy as a slot-free in-combat cast or for a party lacking the spell — easily duplicated by scrolls.

Spiderstep Boots.why: Immunity to webbed and no slowing on web surfaces; entirely encounter-dependent, but you conveniently get them right before one of the few web-heavy fights — wear them there, then swap out.

Swiresy Shoes.why: +1 acrobatics and +5ft jump distance; a passive mobility bump (tripled by Enhanced Leap) that helps low-strength characters cross gaps, but it doubles down on jumping, which most characters already use for mobility.

Varsh Ko'kuu's Boots.why: Acid-surface immunity and acid resistance — a relatively rare resistance, so a fine swap-in for the few acid-themed encounters, but otherwise covered by elixirs/protection spells.

Boots of Apparent Death.why: Feign Death once per short rest; a terrible spell in normal combat, but useful in a couple encounters to keep an allied NPC out of a fight, and a bug lets you knock out and rob merchants without triggering crime.

Boots of Arcane Bolstering.why: 2 turns of Arcane Charge (+2 spell damage vs threatened enemies) on dash; useless for most casters (who won't dash), but works for a Magic Missile evocation-wizard/Rogue and features in theoretical max-damage Spike Growth combos.

Tyrannical Jackboots.why: +1 to Charisma saves (almost never rolled) and Charisma checks; only useful as a swap-in to pass a specific late-game dialogue check from a slot that can't otherwise boost it — more micro than most will do.

Leather Boots.why: Non-magical boots with no mechanical effect — safely ignored.

Metallic Boots.why: Non-magical, +1 Athletics (medium-armor); a negligible bonus worth wearing only before you find any real boots — safe to ignore.

Boots of Genial Striding.why: Immunity to being slowed by difficult terrain (not its damage); difficult terrain is already trivialized by bonus-action jump in BG3, so this rarely matters and better options exist if you want to walk through Plant Growth.

Acrobat Shoes.why: +1 acrobatics and advantage on Dex saves; Dex saves are usually the least threatening (mostly half-damage effects) and you can get this advantage elsewhere — not worth a whole boot slot over mobility/offense.

Dark Justiciar Boots.why: +1 Dex saves and an action-cost, once/rest 60ft teleport to an obscured area; strictly worse than the Night Walkers (bonus action, no restrictions), and spending an action to teleport is rarely worth it.

Blackguard's Greaves.why: +1 Athletics and Longstrider (medium-armor); Longstrider is a ritual spell your party should already have and these don't stack with it, so they're just a worse mobility option — ignorable.

Slinging Shoes.why: A joke returning-weapon: thrown, they deal +2d4 psychic but only 1 base damage (boots are light), so even a throwing build does far less than with a real thrown weapon — funny, not good.

Unwanted Masterwork Greaves.why: Mechanically identical to the Metallic Boots (+1 Athletics) but flagged rare/magical — no reason to pick them up.
